text.title: Fine-tune vs Prompt+RAG Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Fine-tune vs Prompt+RAG Decision Tree application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Before fine-tuning, exhaust: better prompts (+ examples), RAG with rerank, model routing (weak→strong), prompt caching. Fine-tune only after.
rule.rationale: Anthropic + OpenAI guidance + industry consensus: fine-tune solves <30% of problems people try to solve with it; cheaper alternatives solve the other 70%.

rule.statement: Fine-tune fits: style/format consistency, narrow-domain vocabulary, latency reduction via smaller distilled model. Does NOT fit: factual recall (use RAG), reasoning improvement (use prompting/CoT).
rule.rationale: Empirical: fine-tune teaches style; RAG teaches facts; prompting teaches steps. Pick by problem class.

rule.statement: Decision requires baseline eval (≥100 cases) on prompt+RAG. Without baseline, fine-tune ROI is unprovable + the change becomes faith-based.
rule.rationale: Statistical: <100 cases produces ±15pp noise; you cannot distinguish fine-tune lift from variance.

rule.statement: Fine-tuning labor budget = 3× initial estimate. Includes: data prep, eval, retraining loop, deployment. Underestimate is the #1 fine-tune failure pattern.
rule.rationale: Industry case studies (Hugging Face, Cohere, Replicate): fine-tune projects average 2.5-4× initial time estimates.

rule.statement: Decision is revisited on every model major-version change. Today's fine-tune may be cheaper as prompt+caching on next-gen model.
rule.rationale: Frontier models compress fine-tune advantages 6-12 months apart; locked decisions become liabilities.
